IN RESPONSE TO THE COVID-19 OUTBREAK, THE HEARING DATES FOR ALL LAW AND MOTION AND DISCOVERY MATTERS IN DEPARTMENT 302 FILED BEFORE JUNE 1, 2020 ARE OFF CALENDAR PURSUANT TO THE COURT'S GENERAL ORDERS. TO RE-SCHEDULE THE HEARING, PLEASE REFER TO THE PUBLIC NOTICE TO RESTORE SERVICES ISSUED ON MAY 27, 2020 AVAILABLE ON THE COURT'S WEBSITE AT HTTPS://SFSUPERIORCOURT.ORG/. (302/EPS)

LAW AND MOTION, 302, AS TO THE JUN-23-2020 HEARING RE: PLAINTIFFS' MOTION FOR RELIEF FROM DISMISSAL, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING: PLAINTIFFS' MOTION TO VACATE THE DISMISSAL THAT THE COURT ENTERED ON NOVEMBER 20, 2019 IS GRANTED PURSUANT TO THE DISCRETIONARY PROVISION OF CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE 473(B). PLAINTIFFS' APPLICATION IS TIMELY. THIS MOTION WAS FILED WITHIN THE MANDATORY 6-MONTH TIME PERIOD OF THE STATUTE AND PLAINTIFFS HAVE ACTED DILIGENTLY GIVEN THAT THEY HAVE BEEN ATTEMPTING TO RESOLVE THE MATTER INFORMALLY FOR THE PAST FEW MONTHS. PLAINTIFFS DEMONSTRATE MISTAKE, INADVERTENCE, AND/OR EXCUSABLE NEGLECT. THE PARTIES' ORIGINAL "TERM SHEET" AMBIGUOUSLY CONTEMPLATED THAT THE INITIAL PAYMENT WOULD BE MADE WITHIN 90 DAYS OF THE "SIGNED SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT." PLAINTIFFS' UNDERSTANDING THAT THE TIME RAN FROM THE DATE OF EXECUTION OF THE TERM SHEET RATHER THAN THE DATE OF EXECUTION OF THE FORMAL LONG FORM SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT WAS NOT UNREASONABLE. LASTLY, THE COURT REJECTS DEFENDANTS' ARGUMENT THAT DISCRETIONARY RELIEF IS NOT WARRANTED BECAUSE PLAINTIFFS FAILED TO ATTACH A PROPOSED PLEADING. THERE IS NO PLEADING THAT PLAINTIFFS NEED TO FILE FOR THE COURT TO RETURN THIS MATTER TO THE TRIAL CALENDAR. DEFENDANTS' REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS IS DENIED. COUNSEL FOR THE PLAINTIFF IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: SEAN KANE; NOT REPORTED (302/EPS)

LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF EMILY ARREOLA, SERGIO ARREOLA'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT. A R G U E D. COURT HAVING REVIEWED THE PLEADINGS AND HAVING HEARD ORAL ARGUMENT RULES AS FOLLOWS: PLAINTIFFS' MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE A FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT IS GRANTED. CALIFORNIA HAS "A STRONG PUBLIC POLICY OF LIBERAL ALLOWANCE OF AMENDMENTS." (MESLER V. BRAGG MGMT. (1985) 290, 296.) AS DEFENDANTS CONCEDE, "[O]RDINARILY, THE JUDGE WILL NOT CONSIDER THE VALIDITY OF A PROPOSED AMENDED PLEADING IN DECIDING WHETHER TO GRANT LEAVE TO AMEND." (OPP. 2:24-26.) TRIAL CONTINUANCES MUST BE SOUGHT IN DEPARTMENT 206. Off Calendar Jun-23-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: Plaintiffs' Motion For Relief From Dismissal.

No appearances made by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Plaintiffs' motion to vacate the dismissal that the court entered on November 20, 2019 is granted pursuant to the discretionary provision of Code of Civil Procedure 473(b). Plaintiffs' application is timely. This motion was filed within the mandatory 6-month time period of the statute and plaintiffs have acted diligently given that they have been attempting to resolve the matter informally for the past few months. Plaintiffs demonstrate mistake, inadvertence, and/or excusable neglect. The parties' original "term sheet" ambiguously contemplated that the initial payment would be made within 90 days of the "signed settlement agreement." Plaintiffs' understanding that the time ran from the date of execution of the term sheet rather than the date of execution of the formal long form settlement agreement was not unreasonable. Lastly, the court rejects defendants' argument that discretionary relief is not warranted because plaintiffs failed to attach a proposed pleading. There is no pleading that plaintiffs need to file for the court to return this matter to the trial calendar. Defendants' request for sanctions is denied.

Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-22-2019 Overruled
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Matthew Brinegar, Esq. 415-735-6856 of Brinegar Law Firm for Plaintiff.
Thomas Barth, Esq. 916-440-8600 of Barth Daly LLP for Defendant DAHVIE JAMES, PHILIP WATT, FIELD & POND, LLC through Court Call.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT DAHVIE JAMES, PHILIP WATT, FIELD & POND, LLC DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendants' demurrer to the first cause of action of the first amended complaint is overruled. The Consumer Legal Remedies Act ("CLRA") prohibits unfair or deceptive acts undertaken "in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer." (Civ. Code 1770.) The CLRA defines "[s]ervices" as "work, labor, and services for other than a commercial or business use, including services furnished in connection with the sale or repair of goods." (Id. 1761(b).) It defines "goods" as "tangible chattels bought or leased for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes . . . ." (Id. 1761(a).) Among the unlawful practices specifically prohibited by the CLRA are "[m]isrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services" (id. 1170(a)(2)); "[r]epresenting that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that he or she does not have" (id. 1170(a)(5); and "[r]epresenting that a transaction confers or involves rights, remedies, or obligations that it does not have or involve, or that are prohibited by law." (Id. 1170(a)(14).) The CLRA must "be liberally construed and applied to promote its underlying purposes, which are to protect consumers against unfair and deceptive business practices and to provide efficient and economical procedures to secure such protection." (Id. 1760.)

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF EMILY ARREOLA, SERGIO ARREOLA's Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint.

Plaintiffs' motion for leave to file a first amended complaint is GRANTED. California has "a strong public policy of liberal allowance of amendments." (Mesler v. Bragg Mgmt. (1985) 290, 296.) Trial continuances must be sought in Department 206.
